title: Watch 434 Avant-Garde and Surreal Short Films Online: Salvador DalûÙ, Marcel Duchamp, Luis BuûÝuel and Many More
description: Much has been writôÙten lateôÙly about the criôÙsis in HolôÙlyôÙwood, which has left many apparôÙentôÙly sure-fire blockôÙbusters flounôÙderôÙing, theôÙater
body:
Much has been writôÙten lateôÙly about the criôÙsis in HolôÙlyôÙwood, which has left many apparôÙentôÙly sure-fire blockôÙbusters flounôÙderôÙing, theôÙaters empôÙty, and proôÙducôÙtion jobs lost. There are many facôÙtors in play ã some of them, as few diagôÙnoses fail to point out, strucôÙturôÙal ã but can we ignore the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty of fatigue, perôÙhaps even boreôÙdom, with film itself? Weãve postôÙed in recent years here on Open CulôÙture about the decay of cinôÙeôÙma, the rise of ãvisuôÙal muzakã on NetôÙflix,ô why movies donãt feel real anyôÙmore, andô why movies donãt even feel like movies anyôÙmore. Even if theyãve limôÙitôÙed their expoôÙsure to big-budôÙget specôÙtaôÙcles, most once-avid cinephiles will have felt all those pheôÙnomôÙeôÙna for themôÙselves by now, and many will be conôÙsidôÙerôÙing whether to look for a new art form to enjoy. But some will wonôÙder: maybe thereãs a cure?


	

There could well be, and a bracôÙing one. If you seek a re-enchantôÙment with film, there could be few betôÙter places to look than in the work of filmôÙmakôÙers who have broôÙken that mediôÙum down to its very comôÙpoôÙnents and put it togethôÙer again in unconôÙvenôÙtionôÙal ways. Some of the results shocked audiôÙences fifty, sixôÙty, sevôÙenôÙty, even a hunôÙdred years ago ã and indeed, some retain that powôÙer today.
You can take a jourôÙney through the hisôÙtoôÙry of such experôÙiôÙmenôÙtal, avant-garde, and surôÙreôÙal motion picôÙtures with the YouTube playlist at the top of the post, which comôÙprisôÙes 434 such videos. The exact numôÙber will vary dependôÙing upon your region of the world, as well as upon how many of them have come and gone since the playlistãs creôÙation. WhatôÙevôÙer the total, not even a fringe-cinôÙeôÙma habituûˋ will have seen everyôÙthing on it (at least, not more than once).


	

LongôÙtime Open CulôÙture readôÙers may recôÙogôÙnize on the playlist the work of Dadaist Hans Richter and MarôÙcel Duchamp, abstracôÙtion pioôÙneer Viking Eggeling, earôÙly femôÙiôÙnist filmôÙmakôÙer GerôÙmaine Dulac, and aniôÙmaôÙtor (as well as city symôÙphonôÙist) WalôÙter Ruttmann, not to menôÙtion SalôÙvador DalûÙ and Luis BuûÝuel.


	

They may or may not already have encounôÙtered the cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic legaôÙcy of, say, Shé¨ji TerôÙayaôÙma, the all-around avant-gardist and provoôÙcaôÙteur whose influôÙence is still felt in JapanôÙese art today; Stan Brakhage, who forewent even the use of a camôÙera and creôÙatôÙed his own cinôÙeôÙma by manipôÙuôÙlatôÙing film directôÙly; or Michael Snow, whose WaveôÙlength tells a stoôÙry withôÙout leavôÙing a sinôÙgle room in which very litôÙtle hapôÙpens. But then, after enough of these experôÙiôÙmenôÙtal, avant-garde, and surôÙreôÙal viewôÙing expeôÙriôÙences, youãll rememôÙber that there are many ways for a film to tell a stoôÙry ã and much, much more that film can do besides stoôÙryôÙtelling, if only weãd let it.


	

RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
A Page of MadôÙness: The Lost Avant Garde MasôÙterôÙpiece from EarôÙly JapanôÙese CinôÙeôÙma (1926)
The EvocaôÙtiveôÙness of DecomôÙposôÙing Film: Watch the 1926 HolôÙlyôÙwood Movie The Bells Become the ExperôÙiôÙmenôÙtal 2004 Short Film Light Is CallôÙing
Watch the MedôÙiôÙtaôÙtive CinepoôÙem ãH20ã: A LandôÙmark Avant-Garde Art Film from 1929
Watch 3000 Years of Art, a 1968 ExperôÙiôÙmenôÙtal Film That Takes You on a VisuôÙal JourôÙney Through 3,000 Years of Fine Art
Watch MeshôÙes of the AfterôÙnoon, the ExperôÙiôÙmenôÙtal Short VotôÙed the 16th Best Film of All Time
Paul SchradôÙer CreôÙates a DiaôÙgram MapôÙping the ProôÙgresôÙsion of ArtôÙhouse CinôÙeôÙma: Ozu, BresôÙson, Tarkovsky & OthôÙer Auteurs
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: How a Clean, Tidy Home Can Help You Survive the Atomic Bomb: A Cold War Film from 1954
description:
in History |   	April 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Not too far back, we revisôÙitôÙed some Cold War proôÙpaôÙganôÙda that taught upstandôÙing Ame
body:
in History |   	April 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Not too far back, we revisôÙitôÙed some Cold War proôÙpaôÙganôÙda that taught upstandôÙing AmerôÙiôÙcan citôÙiôÙzens How to Spot a ComôÙmuôÙnist Using LitôÙerôÙary CritôÙiôÙcism. Itãs a gem, but it has nothôÙing on the 1954 film,ô The House in the MidôÙdle. SelectôÙedô for preserôÙvaôÙtion in theô NationôÙal Film RegôÙistry by the Library of ConôÙgress, the short docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary makes the ultiôÙmate case for cleanôÙliôÙness. BringôÙing viewôÙers to the NevaôÙda ProvôÙing Grounds, the 12-minute film shows what hapôÙpens when clean, white housôÙes are subôÙjectôÙed to heat waves from an atomôÙic blast, verôÙsus what hapôÙpens when a dingy, ill-kept house goes through the same drill. It turns out that neat peoôÙple can not only claim moral vicôÙtoôÙry (as they always do). They also get to live anothôÙer day. ConôÙsidôÙer it proof of the surôÙvival of the tidiôÙest.
The film was proôÙduced byô the NationôÙal Clean Up-Paint Up-Fix Up Bureau with supôÙport fromô theô FedôÙerôÙal CivôÙil Defense AdminôÙisôÙtraôÙtion. Theô NationôÙal Paint, VarôÙnish and LacôÙquer AssoôÙciôÙaôÙtion also apparôÙentôÙly played a role, sugôÙgestôÙing that corôÙpoôÙrate interôÙests were capôÙiôÙtalôÙizôÙing on wartime fear. Not the first time thatãs hapôÙpened in AmerôÙiôÙca. Or that lastãÎ
If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2013.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Watch Ducked and CovôÙered: A SurôÙvival Guide to the Post ApocôÙaôÙlypse (A LitôÙtle NSFW)
How to Spot a ComôÙmuôÙnistô by Using LitôÙerôÙary CritôÙiôÙcism: A 1955 ManôÙuôÙal from the U.S. MilôÙiôÙtary
AniôÙmatôÙed Films Made DurôÙing the Cold War Explain Why AmerôÙiôÙca is ExcepôÙtionôÙalôÙly ExcepôÙtionôÙal
The Red MenôÙace: A StrikôÙing Gallery of Anti-ComôÙmuôÙnist Posters, Ads, ComôÙic Books, MagôÙaôÙzines & Films

title: 7 Artists We're Excited to See at EXPO CHICAGO 2026
description: From April 9 to 12, EXPO CHICAGO returns to Navy Pier, hosting hundreds of galleries, site-specific projects, talks, and multi-disciplinary progra
body:
From April 9 to 12, EXPO CHICAGO returns to Navy Pier, hosting hundreds of galleries, site-specific projects, talks, and multi-disciplinary programming both downtown and across the city. This week is one of the most exciting times for the Chicago-area art scene, and weãre excited to share our annual preview of what weãre most looking forward to!
Aliza Nisenbaum, ãHitomiã (2022), oil on linen, 66 x 57 inches
1. Aliza Nisenbaum at Anton Kern and Regan Projects
Presented by Anton Kern and Regan Projects, Aliza Nisenbaumãs vibrant portraiture portrays her subjects in bold chromatics. Nisenbaumãs smaller-scale works presented at the fair echo one of her larger projects: a celebratory mural titled ãReading Circles/ Weaving Dreams/ Seeding Futuresãô created for the Obama Presidential Center.
Tawny Chatmon, ãThe Restoration / Made Whole Againã (2024-2025), embroidery and handstitched threadwork on archival pigment print, 30 x 25 inches. Image courtesy of the artist and Galerie Myrtis
2. Tawny Chatmon at Galerie Myrtis
Weãre always excited for the opportunity to see Tawny Chatmonãs portraits up close. Galerie Myrtis will be presenting some of the artistãs newer works, which continue her interest in melding craft techniques with photography. Rather than gold leaf, though, Chatmon embroiders various motifs onto her portraits.
Maya Fuji, ã1PM: CleanôñÕƒÓËÐ´ÌÇÐÍË°ã (2026), acrylic and glitter on canvas, 48 x 60 x 1.5 inches
3. Maya Fuji at Charlie James
Referencing a genre of manga popular in the mid-2000s, Maya Fujiãs Slice of Life peers into the everyday activities of her young characters. Lounging, petting a napping cat, and getting ready for the evening take center stage in these vibrant yet calm paintings.
LaKela Brown, ãTwo Overlapping Collard Leavesã (2026), polyurethane and acrylic, 23 û 8.5 û 2 inches
4. LaKela Brown at 56 Henry
LaKela Brown has traded in her stark white paint for another monochromatic palette, coating her polyurethane sculptures in black. For her solo presentation with 56 Henry, the artist explores ethnobotany and Black life in America through renditions of collard greens, tobacco, corn, chicken wings, and more.
Gerard Mas, ãParty Horn Ladyã (2026), polychrome resin; 53 x 36.5 x 26 centimeters, edition 5 of 7
5. Gerard Mas at Ting Ting Art Space
Longtime Colossal readers will likely recognize this cheeky figure as one of Gerard Masã brazen busts. For the past few years, the Barcelona-based artist has been taking a playful, contemporary approach to sculpture, casting spirited women in a variety of witty roles.
Rahma Lhoussig, ãPlaytime 2ã (2023), mixed technique on canvas, 47 1/5 û 59 3/10 inches
6. Rhama Lhoussig at dmincubator
Merging abstraction and magical realism, Rhama Lhoussig paints vivid domestic scenes in which a recurring figure amuses herself with stuffed bears, flowers, toy blocks, and more. Crayon scribbles and crude line drawings fill the surrounding negative space, firmly placing the works in a dynamic moment of creativity.
Dee Clements, ãlowers, Vase, Basketsã (2026), paper, claybord, reed, pine needle, dye, gouache, water-soluble pastel, 31 x 24.5 inches
7. Dee Clements with Mindy Solomon
Chicagoãs own Dee Clements presents a suite of still-life paintings framed in hand-woven frames, alongside mixed-media sculpture and abstract tapestries.

schema.headline: 7 Artists We're Excited to See at EXPO CHICAGO 2026

title: 12,000 Years Ago, Native Americans Were Playing Games of Chance with Handmade Dice
description: Archaeologists have long known that the ancient peoples of North Americaãnot unlike usãplayed a lot of games. Going back millennia, cultures aroun
body:
Archaeologists have long known that the ancient peoples of North Americaãnot unlike usãplayed a lot of games. Going back millennia, cultures around the world developed myriad ways to keep entertained, and for a long time, it was thought that the first dice ever used could be traced to the ancient Eastern European and Near East cultures of Mesopotamia, the Indus Valley, and the Caucasus. But according to a new paper by Robert Madden, published by Cambridge University Press, games of chance developed much, much earlier than originally thoughtãhalfway around the world.
Researchers previously believed that the earliest dice originated about 5,500 years ago, but Madden shares that examples excavated in North America date back as far as the Late Pleistoceneãthe Ice Age. Among the oldest reported examples are a few found in modern-day Wyoming, Colorado, and New Mexico. The rich archaeological sites in these places are associated with the Folsom Culture, representing a dispersed hunter-gatherer lifeway that extended across the North American West, Southwest, and Great Plains around 12,000 years ago.
Examples of dice with details showing microscopic traces of pigment, with color enhanced for illustration
ãThe dice tend to show up in liminal spaces where you have a lot of high mobility,ã Madden told Live Science. ãIt might have something to do with how separated these people are and the need to relate to people you donãt see very often.ã
In the report, Madden also says that ãthe making and using of dice represent humansã first known efforts to intentionally generate, observe, and record streams of controlled, random eventsãÎã He adds that, possibly for the first time, people were comprehending patterns or regularities in probabilityãa kind of precursor to understanding what we now call the law of large numbers. Anthropologists consider this to be ãa crucial early step in humanityãs evolving discovery and understanding of randomness and the probabilistic nature of the universe.ã
Madden compared hundreds of examples found across the American West with a comprehensive, several-hundred-page publication called Games of the North American Indians, published in 1907 as part of an annual report by the Bureau of American Ethnology. Itãs currently available in a two-volume edition from Bison Books.
You might also enjoy seeing what may be the worldãs oldest crayon.
Illustrations of bone dice from Stewart Culinãs book ãGames of the North American Indians (1907)
Examples of early Native American dice

schema.headline: 12,000 Years Ago, Native Americans Were Playing Games of Chance with Handmade Dice

title: Hillary Waters Fayle Creates 'Portraits of Place' from Seeds, Foliage, and Petals
description: When we think of somewhere weãve been, what are the first things to come to mind? Perhaps there are memorable smells, a sense of other people bein
body:
When we think of somewhere weãve been, what are the first things to come to mind? Perhaps there are memorable smells, a sense of other people being around, or a particular quality of light. But what if we remembered landscapes and experiences through plants? For Hillary Waters Fayle, flower petals, seeds, and foliage combine into a kind of album of various places, which she then uses to create bold cyanotypes.
The artist has long worked with botanicals and other organic materials, notably embroidering foraged leaves and feathers with meticulous geometric designs. With the series Portraits of Place, which sheãs been pursuing for the past six years, Fayle precisely arranges individual petals and leaves into intricate, symmetrical, mandala-like compositions on acrylic.

She starts by collecting and drying botanicals from specific locations, such as Grace Farms Foundation in New Canaan, Connecticut, or Maymont Park in Richmond, Virginia. These are then laid onto watercolor paper thatãs been painted with UV-sensitive iron salts. After being left out in the sun, these result in the bright blue cyanotypes that chronicle the outlinesãperhaps one could even say the spiritãof the distinctive layouts.
ãThe way these portraits illustrate a very particular place and time via botany can be a way to define the relationship that the people of that place have with the landãalmost like a modern-day florilegium,ã Fayle tells Colossal. She continues:

All of these pieces are so different and special to me, but it has been particularly meaningful to make portraits of areas that are going to change drastically in the near future, either from development or rising sea levels, fire, etc. It feels like a way of preserving and honoring the land and all that is present there right now.ô 

Flowers and plants symbolize the natural evolution of particular spaces, such as a gardener adding new bulbs or birds depositing seeds. They symbolize the nature of seasons and life cycles, emphasizing a relationship that is simultaneously enduring and ephemeral. See more on the artistãs Instagram.
Chisman Creek Park, Tidewater, Virginia (detail)

schema.headline: Hillary Waters Fayle Creates 'Portraits of Place' from Seeds, Foliage, and Petals

title: Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds
description:
Announcement
								The first US museum exhibition to focus on the artistãs late work, produced in response to the fascism of the 1930s. On
body:
Announcement
								The first US museum exhibition to focus on the artistãs late work, produced in response to the fascism of the 1930s. On view at the Jewish Museum through July 26, 2026. 

						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Paul Klee, "Angel Applicant (Engel-AnwûÊrter)" (1939), opaque watercolor, brush and black ink, and graphite on paper mounted on board, 25 3/4 û 17 1/2 inches. The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York, The Berggruen Klee Collection, 1984, 1984.315.60. (ôˋ 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)
    
		
					
						Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds traces the Swiss-German artistãs departure from the Bauhaus and his experience throughout the political upheaval of the 1930s prior to his death in 1940. The first US museum exhibition to explore Paul Kleeãs powerful creative output from the final decade of his life, Other Possible Worlds at the Jewish Museum highlights 100 paintings and drawings from across Kleeãs career, including rarely shown works from the 1930s to 1940s. This broader context dramatically frames the artistãs late practice, during which Kleeãs lifelong individuality and imagination prevail as a form of resistance to Nazi ideology and persecution.Born in 1879 in Switzerland to a music teacher and singer, Klee trained in the violin before shifting to the visual arts during his teenage years. He was involved with a range of burgeoning artistic movements during his early career and went on to establish an esteemed reputation during a decade-long tenure at the Bauhaus. In 1931, seeking to free himself from the demands of lecturing and focus on painting, Klee resigned his position and was offered another at the Dû¥sseldorf Academy. However, during Hitlerãs ascent to power, the National Socialists deemed Kleeãs art subversive and degenerate and dismissed him from his position, referring to him as ãa Galician Jew.ã Forced into exile as an immigrant in his country of birth, Klee abandoned his uplifting chromatic style of painting as he confronted the harsh terrain of fascism and soon, in 1935, the effects of scleroderma, a then-fatal autoimmune disease.Paul Klee, ãTropical Blossom (Tropische Blû¥te)" (1920), oil and pencil on primed paper on cardboard, 10 1/4 û 11 5/16 inches. Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern. (ôˋ 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)The exhibition explores this progression, illuminating Kleeãs relentless search for new methods of expressing social critique, non-conformism, mythopoetic thinking, and an evolving approach to developing a new vocabulary for confronting the horrors of political oppression and violence.Curated by Mason Klein, Senior Curator Emeritus, and organized by the Jewish Museum in collaboration with the Zentrum Paul Klee and the Kunstmuseum Bern, Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds is on view at the Jewish Museum, 5th Ave at 92nd St in New York City, through July 26, 2026.Plan a visit at thejewishmuseum.org. Paul Klee, ãUntitled (Last Still Life) (Ohne Titel [Letztes Stilleben])ã (1940), oil on canvas, 39 3/8 û 31 11/16 inches. Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern, Livia Klee Donation. (ôˋ 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)
									
											
												Announcement
											
											
												Exhibition Announcement
											
											
												Sponsored
											
											
												New York
											
											
												Jewish Museum

title: A Dedicated Ruth Asawa Space Is Coming to San Francisco
description:
News
                Complementing the artistãs various public works throughout the city, her family-run estate's forthcoming ga
body:
News
                Complementing the artistãs various public works throughout the city, her family-run estate's forthcoming gallery comes on the centenary of the artist's birth year.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Ruth Asawa drawing at a table in the living room of her Noe Valley home, San Francisco, California, 1985 (photo Paul Glines; artwork ôˋ 2026 Ruth Asawa Lanier, Inc., courtesy David Zwirner)
    
        
            San Francisco is welcoming a new permanent exhibition space for late modernist sculptor Ruth Asawa this coming May, further entwining the artist's legacy of artwork, public activation, and education advocacy within the city on the centenary of her birth year. The space, an extension of the artist's family-run estate, Ruth Asawa Lanier, Inc. (RAL), will open on May 9 at Minnesota Street Project in San Franciscoãs Dogpatch neighborhood with an exhibition curated by two of Asawa's daughters, Addie Lanier and Aiko Cuneo ã both of whom have spent 20 years working on RAL. Referencing the artist's practice of omitting titles for individual works, Ruth Asawa: Untitled will include a variety of rarely exhibited looped wire sculptures for which the artist is best known, as well as cast artworks, paperfolds, watercolor paintings, and drawings on paper and copper foil.Detail of work by Ruth Asawa inô Ruth Asawa: A Retrospective at the Museum of Modern Art in New York last yearô (photo Lisa Yin Zhang/Hyperallergic)Henry Weverka, one of Asawa's 10 grandchildren and president of the estate, told Hyperallergic that the 1,1714-square-foot space is situated on the ground floor and has been outfitted with a redwood wall to give RAL the ãopportunity to display sculpture against dark wood, similar to how it was displayed in Asawa's home.ã ãThe furniture will be inspired by Asawa's house as well, with basic table bases and butcher block tops,ã Weverka continued. ãWe'll also have a replica of Asawa's coffee table with sketchbooks for visitors to sit at and draw in.ãPer the estate, forthcoming exhibitions will continuously bring lesser-known and never-before-seen works by the artist to the public eye, also showcasing them alongside work by Asawa's friends Josef and Anni Albers, Imogen Cunningham, and Ray Johnson, among others. Ruth Asawa, ãUntitledô (PF.686, Watermelon)ã (c. 1960s) (ôˋ 2026 Ruth Asawa Lanier, Inc., courtesy David Zwirner)Asawa, who was held at an incarceration camp for Japanese Americans with her mother and siblings during her teenage years, was later prevented from completing an art teaching degree at the Milwaukee State Teachers College on account of anti-Japanese prejudice.ô Her commitment to fighting for equal access to arts education led her to help found the Ruth Asawa San Francisco School of the Arts in 1982. The new space will also host exhibitions for students and faculty at the arts-aligned alternative public high school, continuing to honor Asawa's role as an arts educator and advocate in the city. ãMinnesota Street Project was built to provide sustainable, long-term space for galleries, artists, and arts organizations, and Ruth Asawa spent her career demonstrating that kind of sustained commitment to San Francisco,ã the organization's executive director, Aimee Le Duc, said in a statement, noting that RAL's incoming presence ãfeels like a natural extension of that shared mission.ãOver a decade after her passing, Asawa continues to leave a mark not only on her home base of over 60 years, but also globally, through various institutional collections and exhibitions. Most recently, the Museum of Modern Art in New York City presented a retrospective of the artist that traveled from the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art. Asawa has also been recognized on an extraterrestrial level, as a crater on planet Mercury was named after her in 2024. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        San Francisco
                      
                      
                        California
                      
                      
                        Ruth Asawa

schema.headline: A Dedicated Ruth Asawa Space Is Coming to San Francisco
